title: Get animated with Figma Motion
speakers: Daniela Muntyan (Product Designer, Figma), Luca Damasco (Manager, Software Engineering, Figma), Dave Hornsby (Product Manager, Figma)
key_points: Figma Motion adds a new 'motion mode' with a timeline, a play head, auto-keyframing, and a properties panel where every animatable property gets a diamond keyframe stamp. Animations play back in real time directly on the canvas.

Two ways to animate: stackable pre-made animation styles (entrances, cycles, exits, ~12-16 presets shipping at launch) and fine-grained keyframes with adjustable easing (presets, custom curves, overshoot, and command-drag for smooth paths).

Deep integration with Figma's design system primitives: animations work with components (color/edits cascade from a parent component down to the final composition), variables (two new types, easing and timing, with modes like standard/expressive/smooth), Draw properties (path trim, arcs, points-on-shape, stroke width), and shader fills.

The Figma agent can generate animations from a prompt, riff on multiple variations, vectorize and animate text, and compose individual shots onto a single staggered timeline, then designers edit the resulting keyframes by hand.

Collaboration features include timestamped comments tied to timeline positions, real-time multiplayer editing with synchronized timelines (spotlight/follow), plus production handoff: a read-only Dev Mode timeline, generated CSS/React Motion/JSON code, MCP prompt export, Figma Make/Weave integration, and MP4/WebM/GIF/animated-SVG export (4K 60fps on Pro Plus). Figma Motion is in beta.

quotes.text: We call him Mo. He's a We're a big fan of Mo.
You're not stuck in your own file on your machine having to copy and paste and send those around for motion, which is just kind of an antiquated workflow.
